title: What's the Role of Meat in a Healthy Diet? -- Dr. Meir ...
text:
foray into a complete different topic
from the one I was assigned I'm assigned
to talk about meat but since I have so
much time to speak i'm going to take on
a completely different topic which is
dietary advice and what can we learn
from the past so 20 years ago the
primary dietary advice was a eat low-fat
and this was bad advice and looking back
reflecting on this well it occurred to
me that there really for at least four
big reasons for why that bad advice got
promulgated so widely and I'll just
touch on each of these the first is
group think i was on the dietary
guidelines committee for two thousand
and full of energy back then and we got
to the fat discussion and so my
colleagues all said well okay thirty
percent calories absolute maximum and I
said wait where's the where's the study
can you point to me one human study that
says that exceeding thirty percent of
calories and fat is bad and they looked
at me like I was from Mars and they said
but all the guidelines say this the
previous guidelines said it the HHA said
it the ACS said it American Cancer
Society heart everybody was saying that
so it obviously must be true and I
persisted and said well you know do they
have access to data that we don't have
and they said okay that's enough we're
not talking about this anymore and moved
on so I think groupthink second is that
this was based on a marker not on
clinical endpoints and the marker was a
bad marker total cholesterol it was
maybe good for 25 years ago 30 years ago
but it's it's really not a good marker
and as dean ornish and others have
commented we really need clinical
outcomes to make
device and that was ignored the third
and probably the biggest issue which
raised by Franken some others is the
desire to be simple and even back then
the advocates of low-fat were saying
well if we advocate low-fat what the
population will do is to decrease
saturated fat which is really what we
want them to do but that message is too
complicated for the consumer so we'll
just say eat low-fat and the desire to
be simple i think was was really a big
big mistake nutrition is pretty simple
but it's not that simple that you can
boil it down to a three word mantra fat
is bad there's good fast bad fats good
carbs bad carbs maybe even good
vegetables and not-so-good vegetables so
it's good to keep it simple but not too
simple and finally I think another key
mistake in retrospect that was made
there was that they ignored the
substitution principle that if you're if
you have a stable weight and a stable
physical activity pattern then your
calories will be roughly stable so if
you eat less of one food you're going to
have to make it up somehow by another
food and I think this was completely
ignored and entirely predictable that
all these fat free products were put on
the market and they were replaced by
refined carbohydrates so you know I
think we can learn from the from the
mistakes of the past and even even in
our current thinking it's easy enough to
think about replacement for nutrients
but we also have to think about that in
the context of food so people say well
is is butter bad and I think the right
answer to that question is compared to
what if you compare it to hi trans
margarine it's good if you compare it to
olive oil it's
add so to categorize foods as good and
bad alone for any food that's providing
calories you have to think of it in
terms of what is replacing it and this
is a key concept it's not rocket science
but it's amazing how commonly it's
ignored that people say this is bad this
is good good compared to what so leads
me to meat is meat bad well let's see so
here's the favorite target of the most
of this group and this is overall us
meat consumption in billions of pounds
since 1900 and it's gone up from 10
billion pounds to say 55 billion this is
a lot of its due to the population
growth this is not per capita its total
but you can see that this is having a
huge impact both on health and the
environment here's the per capita data
and you can see that actually beef
consumption per capita has started to
come down in the last 25 30 years pork
is state about stable chicken's gone up
a lot as was shown in a in an earlier
slide and turkeys going up to I'm not
going to say a lot about the
environmental impact there's lots of
different ways to show this but I hope
by now you're completely convinced that
red meat it takes up a very large
footprint in terms of environmental
impact compared to the alternatives so
health consequences in Western countries
red meat is strongly linked with
increased risk of diabetes cancer
specific death cardiovascular related
death and all cause mortality and I'll
show you a few data slides and this is a
meta-analysis by Susan Larson and Sweden
looking at studies around the world
looking at
different categories of high versus low
consumption of red meat processed meat
and total meat and don't forget this
does not take into account the
substitution this so whatever if the
people who are eating low meet their
eating more of whatever the commish is
of their diet which could be
carbohydrates could be sugar whatever
but you can see that a lot of the points
data points there a point to increase
risk in these various categories I'm
going to skip these what about change in
red meat consumption red meat I'm
talking about mammalian meat so beef
pork lamb if you start low and stay low
you're that's that's the reference
category but if you start low and
increase your red meat consumption risk
gets close to doubling compared to
staying at low levels and there's a
graded response to change in red meat
consumption and diabetes risk this is
the I think the key message when we
think about red meat red meat instead of
what and when we replace red meat with
either low fat dairy knots fish or
poultry you can see these dramatic
reductions in total mortality diabetes
stroke and coronary disease so is meat
bad well compared to these alternatives
yeah you could you could do worse if you
had like a total trans fat diet but and
yet you you also need to take into
account the magnitude of these
differences so when you replace meet
with nuts you see a bigger impact than
some of the other replacement potentials
if you
this slide shows four total mortality
cardiovascular mortality and cancer
mortality red meat for whole grains for
red meat you can see moderate gains in
terms of predicted risk of these
outcomes some studies in Asian countries
have found no association between red
meat and risk cancer specific or all
cause mortality and the potential
explanations are that overall red meat
intake is is on an absolute basis tends
to be lower and it reflects more current
rather than long-term patterns of diet
and it's also related to socioeconomic
status which reflects mortality
experience so the new WHL
classifications processed meat is
carcinogenic to humans that was
considered sufficient evidence and I
agree with that probable carcinogenic
for non-processed red meat and I think
the evidence is pretty good for that as
well so takeaway messages no I'll go to
conclusions I'm going to be on time
dietary protein should be obtained
primarily from plant sources and from
fish and poultry rather than red meat so
15 seconds to spare thank
